questions.question: Explain the difference between Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC).
keyPoints: DAC gives data owners control over who accesses their data; MAC is system-controlled based on security labels
DAC uses GRANT/REVOKE statements; MAC uses classification levels (Top Secret, Secret, Confidential)
DAC is more flexible but less secure; MAC is strict and used in military/government systems
In DAC users can transfer privileges to others; in MAC users cannot change access rights

questions.question: What is Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)? Explain its advantages.
keyPoints: RBAC assigns permissions to roles rather than individual users
Users are assigned roles based on job functions
Advantages: simplified administration, easier permission management, supports principle of least privilege
Reduces complexity as roles can be managed independently of users
Examples: Admin role, Manager role, Clerk role with different access levels

questions.question: Define SQL Injection and explain how to prevent it.
keyPoints: SQL Injection is a code injection attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into queries through user input
Attackers manipulate input fields to gain unauthorized access or modify database data
Prevention methods: use parameterized queries/prepared statements, input validation, stored procedures, least privilege for database accounts
Avoid dynamic SQL generation with user input; sanitize all user inputs

questions.question: Explain the Principle of Least Privilege in database security.
keyPoints: Users should be granted only the minimum privileges necessary to perform their job functions
Helps reduce security risks from compromised accounts or insider threats
Database administrators should have elevated privileges only when necessary
Regular review and audit of user permissions to ensure compliance
Prevents unauthorized access to sensitive data and limits damage from errors or malicious actions

questions.question: How do database views provide security? Give an example.
keyPoints: Views are virtual tables that can restrict access to specific rows or columns of base tables
They hide sensitive data and present only authorized information to users
Example: Create a view showing only employee names and departments, hiding salaries and personal details
Users query the view instead of the base table, gaining controlled access
Views can also aggregate data to prevent exposure of detailed information

questions.question: Explain Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) and its components.
keyPoints: ABAC makes access decisions based on attributes of the subject, resource, and environment
Subject attributes: user role, department, clearance level
Resource attributes: data classification, sensitivity level, owner
Environment attributes: time of access, location, device security status
More flexible and granular than RBAC; policies can be complex and dynamic
Example: Allow access to reports only if user is manager AND department matches AND accessed during work hours

questions.question: What is database auditing? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Database auditing tracks and records all database activities including who accessed what data, when, and what operations were performed
Important for: compliance with regulations (GDPR, HIPAA), detecting unauthorized access, forensic investigation, accountability
Audit logs should capture: user ID, timestamp, operations performed, data accessed, success/failure status
Helps identify security breaches, track policy violations, and support incident response
Should be regularly reviewed and logs protected from tampering

questions.question: Discuss the various access control models used in database security. Compare DAC, MAC, RBAC, and ABAC with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: DAC: Owner-controlled, flexible, but inconsistent security; suitable for less sensitive environments
MAC: System-enforced, strict, high security, but inflexible; used in military/government
RBAC: Role-based, simplifies administration, supports least privilege; scalable but may not handle complex scenarios
ABAC: Attribute-based, highly granular and flexible; complex to implement but very powerful
Comparison: DAC is user-discretionary, MAC is mandatory, RBAC uses roles, ABAC uses dynamic attributes
Selection depends on: organizational needs, sensitivity of data, regulatory requirements, administrative overhead

questions.question: Explain the SQL GRANT and REVOKE statements. Discuss how privileges are managed in a relational database with examples.
keyPoints: GRANT gives specific privileges to users or roles: GRANT SELECT ON employees TO user1
REVOKE removes previously granted privileges: REVOKE INSERT ON employees FROM user1
Privileges include: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, EXECUTE, REFERENCES, ALL PRIVILEGES
Can grant to users or roles: GRANT SELECT ON accounts TO manager_role
With GRANT OPTION allows recipient to grant privileges to others
Example scenarios: Giving read-only access to clerks, write access to managers, all access to admins
Best practices: follow least privilege, regularly audit permissions, revoke unused privileges

questions.question: What is data encryption in databases? Discuss encryption at rest and in transit with its importance in database security.
keyPoints: Data encryption converts plaintext data into unreadable ciphertext using algorithms
Encryption at rest: protects stored data on disk from physical theft or unauthorized access
Encryption in transit: protects data moving between client and server using SSL/TLS
Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) encrypts database files without application changes
Column-level encryption for highly sensitive fields like credit cards, passwords
Importance: protects confidentiality, meets compliance requirements (PCI-DSS, HIPAA), prevents data breaches
Challenges: performance overhead, key management, balancing security with usability

questions.question: Design a comprehensive database access control policy for a banking system. Include discussion of RBAC implementation, authentication, and security measures.
keyPoints: Define roles: Customer Service Rep, Teller, Branch Manager, System Admin, Auditor with distinct permissions
Customer Service: view-only access to customer profiles, no modification rights
Teller: transaction processing privileges (insert/update on transaction tables)
Branch Manager: approve high-value transactions, customer account modifications
System Admin: full database access, user management, no transactional access
Auditor: read-only access to all tables, cannot modify any data
Implement multi-factor authentication, enforce password policies, session timeouts
Use stored procedures for critical operations, row-level security for multi-branch access
Regular access reviews, audit logging of all sensitive operations, incident response plan
